title: Macroscopic evidence of spatial modulation of conductivity in a microtextured ferromagnetic film
authors: C. P. Quinteros, L. Avilés-Félix, D. Goijman, L. Saba, D. Pérez Morelo, L. Granja, M. Granada, J. Milano
abstract: A 75 nm-thick Fe0.5Pt0.5 film is a ferromagnetic metal showing striped magnetic domains in remanence at room temperature. The magnetoresistance is characterized by varying the external temperature and the in-plane magnetic field intensity, thereby affecting its magnetic structure. Qualitatively, the resistivity is well described by using the generalized Ohm's law. High-field magnetotransport properties are successfully explained by considering the competition between the expected metallic behavior and the electron-magnon interaction. In the low-field condition, we size the contribution of the magnetic texture to the macroscopic magnetotransport response by introducing a new quantity. Consistent with the microscopic modulation of the lateral conduction, low-field measurements reveal inhomogeneities attributed to the spatial distribution of ferromagnetic domains and domain walls. By carefully analyzing the macroscopic response near the coercive field, the additional contribution to the resistivity is attributed to the domain walls themselves. In fact, this term could surpass the anisotropic term at low temperatures. In summary, this study demonstrates that spatial magnetic inhomogeneities are not only macroscopically measurable but also comparable in magnitude to other regularly considered terms, mainly at low temperatures.
summary: This work investigates how magnetic domain structures influence the electrical conductivity of a ferromagnetic film. By measuring magnetotransport properties near the material's coercive field, the authors successfully isolate and quantify the additional resistivity contribution arising specifically from the domain walls. This confirms that magnetic inhomogeneities are a significant, measurable factor in the material's electrical response.
main_problem: The study aims to quantify the contribution of spatial magnetic inhomogeneities, such as domain walls, to the macroscopic magnetotransport response in a ferromagnetic film.
main_result: The research demonstrates that the contribution of magnetic domain walls to the resistivity is macroscopically measurable and can be comparable in magnitude to other established magnetotransport terms, especially at low temperatures.
method: Magnetotransport measurements were performed by varying temperature and in-plane magnetic fields, analyzing the longitudinal resistivity ($\rho_L$) near the coercive field.
model_or_system: The physical system is a 75 nm-thick $\text{Fe}_{0.5}\text{Pt}_{0.5}$ ferromagnetic film exhibiting striped magnetic domains. The analysis uses a generalized Ohm's law framework to model the electric field and resistivity components.
key_observables: Longitudinal resistivity ($\rho_L$), transverse resistivity ($\rho_T$), and the resistivity discontinuity ($\Delta\rho_{L,coer}$) near the coercive field.
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), in-plane magnetic field ($H$), and the coercive field ($H_{coer}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on fitting the macroscopic response to theoretical models derived from generalized Ohm's law, assuming the contribution of domain walls can be isolated.
figures_summary: Figures show magnetoresistance ($\rho_L$ vs. $\phi_H$) and resistivity vs. temperature ($T$) curves, highlighting local extrema near $H_{coer}$ and the temperature dependence of resistivity differences.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical framework using generalized Ohm's law to presenting measurements across varying fields and temperatures, culminating in the isolation and quantification of the domain wall contribution to the total resistivity.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the detailed analysis of non-equilibrium transport phenomena and the quantification of mesoscopic structural contributions (domain walls) to bulk properties is relevant to understanding dissipation mechanisms in complex quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Sample details. (a) Hall bar-shaped FePt including the coordinate axes. Longitudinal and transverse directions (along and across the current injection, respectively) are contained within the film plane. The out-of-plane axis is included for completeness. The approximate location of the FePt Hall bar, where the atomic force microscopy was conducted, is sketched by a frame indicated with an arrow. (b)-(d) Atomic force microscopy images of (1µm)2 at room temperature: (b) tapping- mode topography, (c) magnetic, and (d) conductive microscopy maps.

Fig. 1 includes the microscopic characterization of the sample. Figs. 1(b)-(d) comprise images of atomic force microscopy at room temperature (RT) in the central part of the Hall bar (sketched as a rectangle below an arrow in Fig. 1(a)). Fig. 1(b) represents the topography of the sample. Fig. 1(c) displays the phase signal recorded with a magnetic tip (MESPTM from Bruker) in a dual-pass scan (20 nm apart). Finally, Fig. 1(d) illustrates the current detected upon scanning a conductive tip (EtalonTM from NanoTips) in contact mode under the application of 0.5 V. The magnetic signal is uncorrelated to the topography, as expected for the genuine magnetic texture. The MFM

Figure 2: Anisotropic magnetoresistance at room temperature. Longitudinal resistivity (ρL) as a function of the in-plane magnetic field direction (ϕH) measured in saturation (H = 8 kOe). The fitting corresponds to the Voigt-Thomson formula [12], eq. 4.

Figure 3: Longitudinal resistivity (ρL) as a function of T. (a) ρL in saturation (H = 8 kOe) with H//i or H⊥i, left and right axis, respectively.
